data.title: BNY Mellon Core Plus Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
“We expect 2024 to give way to a softer, though still positive year of growth in 2025, of ~2.2%. We expect inflation to trend lower, but progress is likely to remain slow.”

Market Review

President-elect Donald Trump prevailed in the US Presidential election. He won the popular vote, while the Republican Party secured a majority in the Senate and a narrow majority in the House of Representatives.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) cut the Federal Reserve (the “Fed”) funds rate by 25 basis points(bp) in November and again in December, taking it to a range of 4.25% to 4.5%. Chair Powell noted that electing to cut in December was a “closer call”. The central bank adjusted its median “dot plot” interest rate projections to reflect two rate cuts in 2025, down from the four it projected in September. The FOMC increased both its headline and core inflation projections for end-2025 from 2.1% and 2.2% respectively to 2.5%.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended December 31, 2024, the Fund’s Class I shares returned -3.27%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund’s unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, returned -3.06% for the same period.

Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Performance for periods less than 1 year is not annualized. Go to BNY Mellon Investment Management - Global Home - English for the fund's most recent month-end returns. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

Total Expenses (12/31/24)

1 Gross expenses is the total annual operating expense ratio for the fund, before any fee waivers or expense reimbursements. 2 Net Expenses is the total annual operating expense ratio for the fund, after any applicable fee waivers or expense reimbursements. The net Expense ratio(s) reflect a contractual expense reduction agreement through 8/30/2025, without which, the returns would have been lower. The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

Employment data was volatile, distorted by hurricanes and strikes, but continued to display a modest easing of labor market conditions. The unemployment rate started and ended the period at 4.2%. Inflation edged up, with Consumer Price Index (CPI) rising from 2.5% to 2.7% and Personal Consumer Expenditures (PCE) from 2.3% to 2.4%. Core CPI rose from 3.2% to 3.3% and Core PCE from 2.7% to 2.8%. Nonetheless, the crucial “stickier” core services categories, like shelter, transportation and medical services showed some encouraging improvement. Q3 Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth was revised up from 2.8% to 3.1%, with revisions to consumption and net exports the largest drivers.

Following the election, the market initially reacted with a “risk-on move”, with high-beta US equities outperforming and credit spreads rallying, albeit markets retraced some of the rally in December. Political uncertainty relating to potential policies, such as tariffs, helped drive some volatility during the quarter.

Treasuries sold off, underperforming other developed bond markets, with yields rising by up to 83bp. This move fully disinverted the US yield curve. US investment grade credit spreads narrowed by 8bp (albeit they underperformed euro, UK and Australian investment grade markets) for an excess return of 82bp. US high yield markets narrowed by 12bp, for an excess return of 1.2%.

Performance Review

The fund entered the quarter with a modestly long duration and curve steepening bias relative to the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Both detracted as interest rates rose and the US Treasury curve flattened. In plus sectors, relative value trades in inflation and non-US rates also detracted.

Security selection in corporates contributed positively, notably selection in energy and banks. Selection in mortgage-backed securities (MBS) was a slight detractor from relative performance. Overall credit and sector allocation was positive with overweight allocations to high yield and Investment grade credit contributing. From a more granular sector perspective, the overweight allocations to energy and asset-backed securities (ABS) were additive to performance.

The fund entered Q4 having reduced credit risk significantly with the bulk of the reduction having come in midstream energy and junior subordinated banks. After the US presidential election, credit risk was increased in line with an improved outlook for economic growth. Longer duration investment grade credit was slightly decreased in favor of shorter duration high yield creating a net gain in overall credit exposure. At the industry level, the fund focused on additions in utilities, technology, media, and telecommunications (TMT), and food & beverages. Additionally, the fund increased exposure to securitized assets given attractive valuations offered by agency commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), esoteric ABS, and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs). With respect to interest rate positioning, the fund began the quarter positioned for a steeper yield curve and lower interest rates given an outlook for more rate cuts and large, long-term fiscal deficits. The team removed the long duration position early in the quarter and modestly reduced the positioning for a steeper yield curve though we remain focused on the economic implications for long-term rates given the current fiscal environment.

Market Outlook

We expect 2024 to give way to a softer, though still positive year of growth in 2025, of ~2.2%. We expect inflation to trend lower, but progress is likely to remain slow. We believe the incoming administration’s economic agenda creates some uncertainty. Looser fiscal policy is likely to provide a positive impetus to growth, while a potential mass deportation program may do the opposite. Tariffs may have the potential to raise inflation. We expect the Fed is likely to continue its easing cycle, but likely with a slower and more considered approach toward the neutral rate. Rising government bond yields mean “all-in” corporate bonds yields remain attractive relative to history, in our view.

We believe underlying corporate fundamentals remain strong while policy easing and a resilient labor market bode well for credit. Nonetheless, other markets, like European credit can offer relative value, reflecting the softer economic environment.

Global high yield markets appear to be characterized by strong demand. Issuance has been driven primarily by companies refinancing debt due to limited leveraged buy-out activity, leaving investors facing challenges to reinvest proceeds from maturing bonds and higher coupon payments. Given high absolute yields, we expect the favorable technical environment to continue. We expect default rates to remain subdued thanks to resilient growth, better-than-expected earnings, and improved capital market access.

We have adopted a more cautious active stance in the near term. We anticipate significant investment grade supply in H1 2025 as issuers may seek to capitalize on strong demand. This could exert some upward pressure on spreads, creating potential opportunities. Careful security selection and credit research remains critical to ensure that business models remain robust despite higher funding costs.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, or a summary prospectus, if available, that contains this and other information about a fund, contact your financial professional or visit BNY Mellon Investment Management - Global Home - English. Read the prospectus carefully before investing. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

* The BNY Mellon Core Plus Fund ("Fund") commenced operations after the assets of a predecessor mutual fund reorganized into the fund on 2/2/18. Performance for Class Y is the performance from the predecessor fund. The predecessor fund was the Cutwater Investment Grade Bond Fund, Institutional Class, incepted on 12/2/2010, and was renamed to the Insight Investment Grade Bond Fund following BNY’s purchase of Cutwater Asset Management on 1/2/2015. The total return performance figures for Class A and I shares of the Fund represent the performance of the Funds Class Y shares for periods prior to 2/2/18, the inception date for Class A and I shares, and the performance of Class A and I shares, from that inception date. Performance reflects the applicable class distribution/servicing fees since the inception date. Investors should consider, when deciding whether to purchase a particular class of shares, the investment amount, class restrictions, anticipated holding period and other relevant factors. Additionally, on 10/19/2018 the Fund received the merged assets of Dreyfus Intermediate Term Income Fund, the performance of which is not reflected above.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines. The use of derivatives involves risks different from, or possibly greater than, the risks associated with investing directly in the underlying assets. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid, and difficult to value and there is the risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the portfolio will not correlate with the underlying instruments or the portfolio’s other investments. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging-market countries. High yield bonds involve increased credit and liquidity risk than higher-rated bonds and are considered speculative in terms of the issuer’s ability to pay interest and repay principal on a timely basis. Mortgage-backed securities: Ginnie Maes and other securities backed by the full faith and credit of the United States government are guaranteed only as to the timely payment of interest and principal when held to maturity. The market prices for such securities are not guaranteed and will fluctuate. Privately issued mortgage-related securities also are subject to credit risks associated with the underlying mortgage properties. These securities may be more volatile and less liquid than more traditional, government-backed debt securities.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based flagship benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. The index includes Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS and CMBS (agency and nonagency. The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes USD-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility and financial issuers. The Bloomberg High Yield Bond Index measures the USD-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody’s, Fitch and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. Bonds from issuers with an emerging markets country of risk, based on Bloomberg’s EM country definition, are excluded. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Mortgage-Backed Security (MBS) is an investment similar to a bond that is made up of a bundle of home loans bought from the banks that issued them. Investors in MBS receive periodic payments similar to bond coupon payments. Asset-Backed Security (ABS) is a financial security such as a bond or note which is collateralized by a pool of assets such as loans, leases, credit card debt, royalties, or receivables. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, such as transportation, food, and medical care. A yield curve is a line that plots yields, or interest rates, of bonds that have equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country’s borders in a specific time period. Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) also known as consumer spending, is a measure of the spending on goods and services by people of the United States. A yield curve is a line that plots the yields or interest rates of bonds that have equal credit quality but different maturity dates. Investment grade refers to the quality of a company's credit. To be considered an investment grade issue, the company must be rated at 'BBB' or higher by Standard and Poor's or 'Baa" or higher by Moody's. Sticky is a general economics term that can apply to any financial variable that is resistant to change. In finance, esoteric refers to investments that are complex, unconventional, and not well understood. These investments are often less liquid and can be riskier than more traditional investments.

This material has been provided for informational purposes only and should not be construed as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular investment product, strategy, investment manager or account arrangement, and should not serve as a primary basis for investment decisions. Prospective investors should consult a legal, tax or financial professional in order to determine whether any investment product, strategy or service is appropriate for their particular circumstances. Views expressed are those of the author stated and do not reflect views of other managers or the firm overall. Views are current as of the date of this publication and subject to change. This information contains projections or other forward-looking statements regarding future events, targets or expectations, and is only current as of the date indicated. There is no assurance that such events or expectations will be achieved, and actual results may be significantly different from that shown here. The information is based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate and may be superseded by subsequent market events or for other reasons. References to specific securities, asset classes and financial markets are for illustrative purposes only and are not intended to be and should not be interpreted as recommendations.

Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission.

Investment advisory services in North America are provided by Insight North America LLC, a registered investment adviser and regulated by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Insight North America LLC is associated with other global investment managers that also (individually and collectively) use the corporate brand Insight Investment and may be referred to as "Insight" or "Insight Investment." Insight is a subsidiary of The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation.

data.title: Herc Holdings: Weak Local Account Demand Expected To Temper Short-Term Growth
data.description:
The Thesis

As this equipment rental supplier, Herc Holdings (NYSE:HRI), exited the year, it delivered double-digit growth across its topline due to robust performance, particularly in its national accounts business, which benefited from healthy megaproject activity throughout 2024. However, the local account growth remains subdued due to the continued softer demand environment. I expect the company's overall topline growth to remain under pressure in FY25 as the elevated interest rate environment across the region continues to impact the local project activity negatively, impacting the demand for the company's rental services. In my view, this continued volume pressure across most of the company's business should also impact the margin growth in the quarters ahead, despite the recent single-digit price increase. Since my last bullish rated article on HRI, the stock is up decently, around 30%. While I remain optimistic about the company's long-term growth prospect due to infrastructure spending tailwinds and its focus on acquisitions and efficiency improvements, its near-term outlook appears weak, which along with the stocks' valuation near historical levels, I am downgrading HRI to a HOLD for now.

Last Quarter Highlights

After delivering single digit growth throughout the year, the company's consolidated topline was up in high teens as it exited 2024, primarily due to rental revenue growth driven by mega projects, contribution from recent acquisitions and easier year-on-year comparison with single digit growth in the prior year's quarter. The company's topline grew approximately 14.4% year-on-year reaching $951 million, with an organic growth of about 6.5% in the fourth quarter of 2024. For the full year 2024, the company's consolidated topline grew 8.7% versus FY23 to $3.6 billion, primarily due to moderate growth in the initial quarter of the 2024.

During the quarter, while the volume remains healthy, the benefit from favorable pricing and cost discipline helped the company, offsetting the impact from reduced fleet efficiency in slower local market demand. As a result, HRI reported a nearly flat growth in its adjusted EBITDA margins to 46.1% during the quarter from 46% a year ago. EBITDA growth during the quarter also benefited the company's bottom-line growth as its adjusted EPS increased to $3.58 from $3.24 a year ago, however, missed the consensus estimates by $0.36 in the fourth quarter of FY24.

Exiting the year, HRI saw double-digit growth in the last quarter after experiencing moderate single digit growth throughout 2024. While the company continued to outpace the overall rental market primarily due to its robust national accounts business led by mega projects, the local market demand environment remains soft due to elevated interest rate environment across the region. Considering a significant share of the local account business of nearly 54% in the company's total revenue, I expect the continued headwind for this segment due to high interest-rate environment should put pressure on the overall topline growth in the quarters ahead as it continues to influence customers spending decisions leading to reduced new projects starts.

However, the national account business is expected to remain healthy due to strong funding and opportunities from infrastructure projects on more mega projects related to LNG, data centers, semiconductors, healthcare and education which are expected to ramp up in 2025. In my view, this along with projects initiated in the latter half of 2024 along with benefit from the recent rental rate increase should support the topline growth in 2025, partially offsetting the impact from weak local market demand. As a result, despite robust activity across the national account business, I expect the company to deliver its topline growth in mid to low to mid-single digits in the quarters ahead, in line with the company's guidance of 4%-6% of rental revenue growth for FY25.

Although, the pressure from soft demand in the local business is expected to continue in the short term, the long term remains favorable as project pipeline remain robust. With nearly $357 billion in infrastructure projects being forecasted for 2025 and many more in the next 3 years, HRI appears to be only at the beginning of a strong activity period in the industry in the coming years. HRI is targeting to capture 10% to 15% of these opportunities driven by significant investments, which should support the company's national account business in FY25 and beyond. Apart from this, the company has also added nearly 51 businesses and 81 greenfield locations through acquisition in the past few years, which are anticipated to contribute meaningfully to overall revenue growth as they mature in the next 2-3 years.

Overall, the company's long-term prospects remain promising due to tailwinds from significant infrastructure spending on mega projects in the coming years, particularly related to LNG, data centers, and semiconductors, which, along with HRI's diversified market positions across various end markets and its continued focus on market share expansion through strategic M&As and investment in new equipment, should drive topline growth in the coming years. However, talking about the short-term demand environment, the company continues to face pressure from subdued growth in its local accounts business due to lower project activity and discouraged customer spending by elevated interest rates, which are expected to largely offset the continued growth in the national account business, impacting the overall topline growth in FY25.

Valuation and Takeaway

I last covered HRI in September last year with a bullish view due to decent short-term growth prospects and an attractive valuation multiple. Since that article, the stock has grown nearly 30%, significantly outperforming its peers stock performance. Currently, the company's stock is trading at a non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 15.21, based on its FY25 EPS estimates of $13.13, representing a low single digit increase from a year ago. In comparison to its historical average multiple of 16.37x, the stock appears to be trading at a slight discount. While the stock looks a bit cheaper than its five-year average P/E, its forward growth has also moderated significantly to just mid-single digit as compared to the strong double-digit growth it has experienced in the past years, as can also be seen in the company growth table below.

While the company appears to be strongly positioned to benefit from a robust mega project pipeline driven by significant infrastructure spending in the coming years, the near term demand environment remains soft. In my view, the company's local account business should remain under pressure going forward as the elevated interest rate environment continues to affect the project starts due to discouraged customer spending decisions across the region.

I believe this should result in continued volume pressure in the local accounts business, which accounts for over 54% of total revenue share, further impacting the margins and bottom line in the short term. The company's interest burden has also increased slightly due to increased debt to fund the acquisition completed in 2024, which should also impact the company's bottom-line performance, further deteriorating the stock valuation in the quarters ahead. Therefore, despite a favorable long-term outlook, uncertainty in the near term due to a softer demand environment across most of the business and a valuation multiple near the historical average suggests staying on the sidelines for now. Hence, I am downgrading the stock to a "HOLD" rating for now.

Source: FactSet. As of 12/31/2024. Past performance is not a reliable indicator or guarantee of future results. Due to market volatility, the market may not perform in a similar manner in the future. Indexes are unmanaged, do not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and are not available for direct investment. The index data provided is not representative of any Lord Abbett product.

Market Review

  • In the fourth quarter, U.S. Treasury yields rose significantly, and the yield curve steepened, resulting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index ending the quarter down over 3.0%, while U.S. equities experienced positive returns, led by a strong rally in large cap growth stocks. Markets were driven by the November presidential election, which saw a surge in investor optimism due to expectations of stronger economic growth, improved profit forecasts, and a more favorable regulatory environment. There were several bearish factors, however, including uncertainties regarding the incoming administration's policy proposals, debt and deficit concerns, and strong dollar headwinds. Economic data was mixed, with a rebound in November nonfarm payrolls but stalled progress on inflation. The U.S. Federal Reserve (the Fed) cut rates by 25 basis points in November and December. Although, the Fed adopted a more hawkish stance, projecting fewer rate cuts for 2025 than previously anticipated.[1]
  • Credit sectors performed well over the quarter, as investment grade corporate[2] spreads tightened 10bps, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS)[3] spreads tightened 24 bps, and asset-backed securities (ABS)[4] spreads tightened by 20 bps. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.64% to 4.24% and the 10-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.79% to 4.57%.[1]

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned -2.78%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended December 31, 2024. The Fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index*, returned -3.06% during the same period.
  • Duration positioning contributed to relative performance over the quarter. Being underweight duration versus the benchmark contributed as U.S. Treasury rates moved higher during the period.
  • Security selection within investment grade corporate bonds contributed to relative performance, specifically within the Financials sector. We reduced the portfolio's overall exposure to investment grade corporate bonds, rotating into other sectors we believe offer better relative value.
  • The portfolio's allocation to ABS and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) modestly contributed to relative performance. Within CLOs, we increased the portfolio's exposure, adding to AAA-rated CLOs. Within ABS, we modestly reduced our overall exposure.
  • The portfolio's allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) detracted from relative performance. We increased the portfolio's exposure to MBS as we believe this is an attractive asset class for high quality carry and it may perform well as rate volatility declines.
  • There were no other significant detractors over the period.

Asset Class Distribution

Footnotes

* The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is an index of U.S dollar-denominated, investment-grade U.S. government and corporate securities, and mortgage pass-through securities, and asset-backed securities. Indexes are unmanaged, do not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and an investor cannot invest directly in an index.

1 Factset as of 12/31/2024

2 As represented by the ICE BofA US Corporate Index as 12/31/2024

3 As represented by the Bloomberg US CMBS Index as of 12/31/2024

4 As represented by the Bloomberg US Aggregate Securitized ABS Index as 12/31/2024

5 Intermediate-term core bond portfolios invest primarily in investment-grade U.S. fixed-income issues including government, corporate, and securitized debt, and hold less than 5% in below-investment-grade exposures. Their durations (a measure of interest-rate sensitivity) typically range between 75% and 125% of the three-year average of the effective duration of the Morningstar Core Bond Index.

A Note about Risk: The Fund is subject to the general risks associated with investing in fixed income securities, including market, credit, liquidity, and interest rate risk. The value of an investment in the Fund will change as interest rates fluctuate in response to market movements. When interest rates rise, the prices of debt securities are likely to decline, and when interest rates fall, the prices of debt securities tend to rise. The Fund may invest in high yield, lower-rated debt securities, sometimes called junk bonds and may involve greater risks than higher rated debt securities. These securities carry increased risks of price volatility, illiquidity, and the possibility of default in the timely payment of interest and principal. The Fund may invest in foreign or emerging market securities, which may be adversely affected by economic, political, or regulatory factors and subject to currency volatility and greater liquidity risk. The Fund may invest in derivatives, which are subject to greater liquidity, leverage, and counterparty risk. These factors can affect Fund performance.

The Fund's portfolio is actively managed and is subject to change.

The credit quality of the securities in a portfolio are assigned by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO), such as Standard & Poor's, Moody's, or Fitch, as an indication of an issuer's creditworthiness. Ratings range from 'AAA' (highest) to 'D' (lowest). Bonds rated `BBB' or above are considered investment grade. Credit ratings 'BB' and below are lower-rated securities (junk bonds). High-yielding, non-investment-grade bonds (junk bonds) involve higher risks than investment-grade bonds. Adverse conditions may affect the issuer's ability to pay interest and principal on these securities.

The performance table above is based on Class I shares. Average Annual Total Returns are based on changes in the net asset value and assume reinvestment of all distributions, and do not reflect deduction of any front-end sales charges which are not applicable for Class I shares. Returns for less than one year are not annualized.

Bloomberg Index Information:

Source: Bloomberg Index Services Limited. BLOOMBERG® is a trademark and service mark of Bloomberg Finance L.P. and its affiliates (collectively "Bloomberg"). Bloomberg owns all proprietary rights in the Bloomberg Indices. Bloomberg does not approve or endorse this material, or guarantee the accuracy or completeness of any information herein, or make any warranty, express or implied, as to the results to be obtained therefrom and, to the maximum extent allowed by law, shall not have any liability or responsibility for injury or damages arising in connection therewith.

ICE BofA Index Information:

ICE Data Indices, LLC ("ICE"), used with permission. ICE PERMITS USE OF THE ICE BofA INDICES AND RELATED DATA ON AN "AS IS" BASIS, MAKES NO WARRANTIES REGARDING SAME, DOES NOT GUARANTEE THE SUITABILITY, QUALITY, ACCURACY, TIMELINESS, AND/OR COMPLETENESS OF THE ICE BofA INDICES OR ANY DATA INCLUDED IN, RELATED TO, OR DERIVED THEREFROM, ASSUMES NO LIABILITY IN CONNECTION WITH THE USE OF THE FOREGOING, AND DOES NOT SPONSOR, ENDORSE, OR RECOMMEND LORD ABBETT, OR ANY OF ITS PRODUCTS OR SERVICES.

An asset-backed security (ABS) is a type of financial investment that is collateralized by an underlying pool of assets-usually ones that generate a cash flow from debt, such as loans, leases, credit card balances, or receivables.

Mortgage-backed securities are variations of asset-backed securities that are formed by pooling together mortgages exclusively.

Commercial mortgage-backed securities are fixed-income investment products that are backed by mortgages on commercial properties rather than residential real estate.

A collateralized loan obligation (CLO) is a single security backed by a pool of debt. Collateralized loan obligations (CLO) are often backed by corporate loans with low credit ratings or loans taken out by private equity firms to conduct leveraged buyouts.

The views and information discussed in this commentary are as of December 31, 2024, are subject to change, and may not reflect the views of the firm as a whole. data.title: Trump-Era Tariffs: Impact On Trade And Emerging Market Bonds
data.description:
Transcript

Eric Fine - So what's happened with tariffs under the new Trump administration? Well, tariffs were announced on Canada and Mexico at an expected 25%. But more or less immediately, within 24 hours, got a one-month extension in exchange for some strategic concessions, not on economic issues.

Tariffs were also announced on China at 10%. But the response was number one targeted, and number two didn't include soybeans. So these are indications that they're saving those issues for a compromise later.

And overall, you could argue that the Trump administration's response has been sensitive to the market impact of China. It is a tit-for-tat game, unlike the potentially one-off 'chicken' games with Mexico, Colombia, and Panama. But it seems bounded. And the Chinese response was consistent with that view.

Last but most importantly, the Chinese foreign exchange fix, the CNY fix, is their most important signaling mechanism for months now. It has been stable to better than expected by the survey the morning before the fix. It's a really strong sign of stability and of calm. It could be held up as a threat if negotiations go bad. But if the negotiations between the US and China end up with 10% tariffs and a 10% CNY deval, even Treasury Secretary Bessent is going to say that's a loss.

So we think one of the modalities, probably central case, is an agreement on currency stability with the US, whatever gets agreed on tariffs. And that's important because that's the only thing you can really care about in bonds, is how does this transmit?

If the FX is stable, we expect the same thing we've seen for last five years, which is Asia gets anchored. Remember, Asian interest rates are now the same as US interest rates, broadly speaking. EM Asian is the biggest thing that's happened in last five years, and that will get anchored. So that's what's happened, and the market is trying to figure out what stage are we at, and how are things going to be going forward.

So I told you what happened in terms of the Trump administration's tariffs and the response from China. What does this mean for asset prices in general? Well, I think there are two, maybe three big implications.

The first is interest rates down. Why? It's the biggest short in the market still. And a lot of the concerns are projections, in our opinion, of fiscal concerns on Trump that say more about the speaker than about reality. There may be fiscal concerns about Trump that are legitimate. We think it's way too early, and we think it's more or less a hollow argument.

The other one is potentially dollar down. It's the second-biggest position in the market, particularly against euro, some against CNY, CNH, some still against dollar peso. And that could reverse, especially in the case of Mexico. But that's a second big implication is dollar down.

The third is, and this is a little more speculative, it might be risk on. To the extent that these concerns that the market has had, not just since President Trump's election, but before Trump's election, may be peaking, right? Either peaking or resolved. And that will be the elimination of a big period of uncertainty. And what are you going to be left with if this is gone? What you're going to be left with is a US that's stimulating. Remember, taxes are being cut, regulations are being cut, that's stimulative. And China is at risk of stimulating.

The property sector has been a really interesting sector for us, but the bottom line of it is we think a lot of the major issues in the property sector may be over. What over means for us is we don't find cheap things there anymore. The majors are established.

So this is a pretty neat context. So I summarized what the Trump administration has done on tariffs. I told you what I think it means for asset prices in general, what does it mean for our portfolio of EM bonds in particular.

For months, we have been trying to establish a position that reflects what will happen once these risks are either priced or addressed. And the main way we had done that was having an overweight in local currency. Local currency overall did poorly, however, not if you didn't own Mexico.

So our number one response will continue to be bottom-up finding the countries, not some top-down response. So we're very, very selective. And last year, our best performer was not owning Mexico. And in January, our best performer was owning Mexico. And so we think that's one really important implication is country-specific.

So Mexico is a big part of our answer, but the real answer is country by country. This may have been a game of chicken for Mexico, Colombia and Panama. And the most important feature of a game of chicken is that it's only one round. And that one round may be over. We don't know yet.

Another big part of our answer is going to be long duration. So we have an overweight relative to our benchmark and duration. But real rates in a lot of EMs were especially high, both policy rates and market rates. So you could see this duration rally really be turbocharged in some of the EM local currency bond markets, as well as their long-dated dollar-denominated bonds. So we have some century bonds that have a lot of upsides if rates go down and not a lot of downsides if rates go higher.

So those are our main reactions. We're maintaining our attractiveness to duration and continuing to have exposure to the world as it's going to look when this is priced or resolved.

Important Disclosures

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified Index: The J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified tracks local currency bonds issued by Emerging Markets governments.

J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified EMEA Index: J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified EMEA Index tracks local currency bonds issued by European, Middle Eastern, and African Emerging Markets governments.

J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified Latin America Index: J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified Latin America Index tracks local currency bonds issued by Latin American Emerging Markets governments.

J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified Asia Index: J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified Asia Index tracks local currency bonds issued by Asian Emerging Markets governments.

J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified Asia ex-China Index: J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified Asia ex-China Index tracks local currency bonds issued by Asian Emerging Markets governments excluding China.

data.title: Black Stone Minerals' Double-Digit Yield Looks To Benefit From Expanding Electricity Demand
data.description:
With a distribution yield hovering around 10%, Black Stone Minerals, L.P. (NYSE:BSM) is in a position to benefit from the current administration's more favorable approach to fossil fuels and by extension, BSM's ownership of the rights to the fossil fuel minerals on its land. At the same time, this upstream master limited partnership (MLP)'s key commodity, natural gas, is experiencing a healthier supply/demand balance that should incentivize increased production. Greater natural gas volume means more royalty income from BSM's mineral ownership, as well as leasing demand from its E&P customers. Since 2023, excess supply and low prices for natural gas have kept BSM range-bound. However, changing macro energy priorities, in combination with an improving natural gas health, have increased the potential for distribution hikes as well as unit price appreciation. As shifting supply/demand dynamics unfold, Black Stone Minerals' offers investors a robust distribution. It is a buy for income seekers able to tolerate the variable nature of its distribution approach.

BSM's History Of Adaptation And Surviving

Black Stone Minerals seems to be the exception among upstream MLPs -- a subset of the MLP universe with a checkered past. Some readers may recall Linn Energy: Founded in 2003 by Michael Linn, the company's success incentivized an MLP IPO in 2012 after Linn had retired. Four years later, Linn Energy was filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy, primarily due to poor capital discipline that resulted in too much debt. Vanguard Natural Resources is another example: Called the "perfect MLP" in 2015, this E&P MLP filed for bankruptcy twice (in 2017 and 2019) and the private LLC that emerged (Grizzly Energy) ultimately auctioned off its wells.

In contrast, Black Stone's IPO as an MLP in 2015 was preceded by close to 50 years of energy experience and over 130 years as an on-going natural resource company. The current CEO and chairman of BSM is the great-grandson of the MLP's predecessor company, W.T. Carter & Brothers, founded in 1876. Originally, this family-operated company's focus was acquiring land to grow and harvest timber as well as manufacture lumber. After a century-plus of profitable operations, in the late 1960s, the Carter family's focus changed from harvesting trees on its real estate to what was below the surface; namely, the minerals extracted from the earth (formed by fossil decomposition, hence the term fossil fuels) that become the fuel minerals for oil and natural gas.

A Third-Generation CEO's Influence and Foresight

CEO Thomas L. Carter, Jr. has been intricately involved with Black Stone's evolution within the oil and gas business for over four decades. Having founded Black Stone Energy Company in 1980, a subsidiary of his father and grandfather's business, Carter's was instrumental in attracting institutionally-backed private investment funds and shifting focus to a mineral and royalty business model. His success for investors led to taking the mineral rights business public in 2015, despite timing that occurred in the middle of an oil and gas slump. At the time, Carter said he was taking the long-term approach of raising capital through the IPO with an eye toward making acquisitions during the down cycle. A durable approach to an industry know for seismic cyclicality continues to characterize Black Stone Minerals.

Decades of Experience Has Bred Discipline

Understanding the inevitable depletion of timberland inventory led the original lumber business to an early adoption of forest conservation principles. A forward thinking approach was unique during lumber's "Bonanza Era" from the 1880s through the 1930s. This allowed the company to outlast the majority of its contemporaries and eventually shift to the booming resource extraction industry of oil and gas. An analogy to BSM's current-day methodical, conservative approach seems fitting.

No Debt and Ample Liquidity

Black Stone Minerals' balance sheet is essentially debt-free, having consistently paid it down -- even during Covid's impact-- to today's zero balance, as seen below. The royalty business model shields BSM from the high capital and operating expenditures typical of E&P companies, giving the MLP a big advantage in the boom/bust upstream space. BSM's low-leverage priority differs quite favorably from the past upstream MLPs previously mentioned.

Additionally, BSM has an untapped credit facility with an aggregate maximum credit amount of $1.0 billion. In short, liquidity is ample.

A+ Profitability and Plentiful Cash Flow

Seeking Alpha's A+ profitability rating reflects BSM's 91% EBITDA margin, but it is operational cash flow that guides the MLP's quarterly distribution. As stated in their 10-K, BSM's cash flow from operations dictates its distribution:

(the distribution) each quarter principally depends upon the amount of revenues we generate, which are largely dependent upon the prices that our operators realize from the sale of oil and natural gas...(However) the amount of cash we distribute to holders of our units depends primarily on our cash generated from operations and not our profitability."

On a per share basis, the graph below shows cash from operations (the orange line) juxtaposed to the distribution (the purple line) along with EPS (the blue line). Black Stones' revenues will vary as E&P lessees react to their commodities price dynamics, but BSM has managed to maintain healthy profitability and adequate operational cash for distribution over time.

Morningstar calculates the 5-yr average of BSM's free-cash-flow as a percentage of revenues to be 71%, reflecting management's cash flow preservation priority. Additionally, there's an active hedging strategy to shield cash flow from weak energy price and dampen volatility. BSM layers its hedges over time, which can both offset price declines and limit upside when prices increase.

A Variable But Rewarding Distribution Over Time

Black Stone's business model doesn't lend itself well to a fixed distribution due, in part, to its interconnectedness with E&P production levels subject to change. Tying the distribution to operational cash flow maintains balance sheet strength and enables flexibility for opportunistic acquisition of mineral interests. This variable distribution approach isn't for investors seeking payout stability, but if able to ride its ups/downs, BSM's record instills confidence. As seen below, when industry conditions are favorable, BSM can greatly benefit. over the five years since 2020's lowered distribution, the MLP annualized a total return close to 19%.

Black Stone Minerals has never not paid a distribution. However, as seen above, the distribution's slow, steady climb higher after the IPO was disrupted in 2020-2021 by pandemic-related conditions. The following two years saw the distribution raised to its highest levels ever. But last year, excess natural gas storage levels resulted in a key operator's decision (Aethon) to enact its “time-out” provision regarding production volume. This resulted in BSM shaving the distribution by 10 cents (~20%). At the time, CEO Carter affirmed a full reinstatement on the Q1 earnings call:

we have set our plan to grow the distribution back to the highwater level mark by 2026 through production growth alongside liquefied natural gas demand that is expected to drive higher natural gas prices."

Many E&P companies adopt a variable payout approach but few distributions have as much robustness as BSM's. With improving natural gas demand (both realized and expected) the distribution's return to its previous level may be sooner than 2026.

Acreage Representing Decades of Inventory

After its largest acquisition in 2017 from Noble Energy for their mineral and royalty assets, BSM has pursued accretive "smaller mineral packages that complement an existing position or establish a foothold in an emerging play." That foothold, represented below, is extensive enough to offer a long runway of extraction inventory for third-parties to lease.

Examples of lessees include: Aethon, EOG Resources(EOG), XTO Energy(XTO), Expand Energy(EXE), Diamondback Energy(FANG), Continental Resources(CLR), Hess(HES), ConocoPhillips(COP), Apache(APA), ExxonMobil(XOM), Occidental Petroleum(OXY), and others. This long list makes sense given BSM's geographic reach is spread across the most productive onshore basins: Texas' Permian, North Dakota's Bakken and Louisiana's Haynesville. Its Gulf-related basins' proximity to LNG export terminals should prove especially helpful, given management's expectation to benefit from increased LNG exports.

It's noteworthy that Black Stone is using its 20 million acres in other energy-related ways. In August of 2023, it signed an agreement with Longroad Energy to provide the renewable company with exclusive access to several thousands of Black Stone's acres across 12 states to develop and operate large-scale solar farms, all the while preserving Black Stone's ability to co-develop its mineral and subsurface rights.

Navigating E&P's Fluctuating Production

As mentioned, the production volume of upstream E&P companies leasing their acreage is pivotal to Black Stone. Management works closely with its operators, as well as monitoring industry rig counts and natural gas storage levels. The latter is especially important given production levels are heavily influenced by storage levels. In 2023 natural gas inventory was 25% above its 5-yr average and jumped to 40% above this average last March. Accordingly, spot prices dropped below Covid levels to $1.50/mcf (3/2024) contributing to BSM's major lessee, Aethon's time-out provision decision and the partnership lowering its distribution last spring.

Recent colder-than-expected temperatures have meant above-average inventory withdrawals, propelling the spot price's rebound 135% higher since last March's lows to mid-Feb's $3.94/MMBtu. The graph below shows natural gas inventory's pattern of cyclicality and why low leverage is a priority to BSM.

Another depiction of the current supply/demand balance is shown below. It's a visual representation of natural gas's demand starting to surpass supply last fall.

At a Goldman Sachs conference earlier in January, two of BSM's top operators mentioned the changing dynamic for natural gas. Specifically, the CEOs of Aethon and Expand Energy (the result of Chesapeake Energy buying Southwestern Energy) see their markets remaining tight for another 2.5 years. Incentive to produce significantly more kicks in when prices near the $5/MMBtu, which natural gas future prices are predicting.

The Right Fuel At The Right Time

Energy's boom/bust nature will continue. However, seeing insufficient energy, especially electricity, as a threat to the Nation’s economy, national security, and foreign policy and, in turn, declaring a "national energy emergency" adds new pieces to the power generation puzzle. The departments of Energy, Interior, EPA and even Defense, have all been authorized to slash regulations and improve permitting for extraction and power plants; end the pause on LNG exports and approve permits for its infrastructure; streamline pipeline permitting; cut GHG regulations, and make electricity a national defense priority. On a federal level, this is a very different direction than prior goals (e.g., zero-carbon regulation, ESG mandates, etc.). This change is likely to bring increased production on BSM's acreage resulting in more royalties, improved lease terms and bonuses, as well as augmented LNG exporting.

Critics of the policy shift rightly point out the current high levels of production. On the other hand, international regulatory bodies like the North American Electric Reliability Corp (NERC) validates the concern when it comes to electricity. Their Dec 2024 report summarized the problem this way:

Electricity peak demand and energy growth forecasts over the 10-year assessment period continue to climb higher than at any point in the past two decades...reversing an almost two-decade trend of falling or flat growth rates.

Grid reliability has been a worry for years. Now, data center power demand (for both AI and crypto), generator retirements (e.g. coal-based), and the 'electrification' of everything has NERC calling for much more natural-gas fired power plant generation. Currently, natural-gas-fired power generates 42% of the electrical energy consumed. Variable resources (wind and solar) cannot provide the baseload power needed and nuclear energy's high cost to build, radioactive waste, and other concerns, all combine to highlight natural gas' advantages. LNG exporting gets lots of attention when anticipating natural gas's future but, equally important will be its role in domestic power generation to meet the exploding electricity demand and the need for baseload reliability. NYNEX forward monthly pricing (as of mid-Feb) was $4.95/MMBtu for next January and $4.85/MMBtu in January 2027 reflecting, in part, expectation of natural gas' power generation role.

Black Stone Compared To Chevron and Exxon Mobil

BSM's is relatively unknown and not given a lot of attention by Wall St analysts. Its $3.2B market capitalization pales in comparison to behemoths like ExxonMobil(XOM) and Chevron(CVX). However, comparing BSM to Chevron and Exxon's performance is instructive (per the chart below). On a total return basis over the past decade, Black Stone performed slightly better than Exxon and not far behind Chevron. Since 2015, BSM annualized 6.8%, XOM returned 6.7% and CVX earned 8.3%. This performance backs up Black Stone's management's characterization that they blend the energy and speed of a small family-owned business with the resources of a larger company.

A Management Aligned with Unitholders That's Buying

Insider ownership suggests a strong alliance with unitholders. As seen below, close to 16% of the outstanding shares are held by insiders, CEO Carter being the largest holder. Interestingly, over a dozen of these insiders have been adding to their positions in January. (One of these insiders with upstream MLP experience is Michael Linn, founder of the previously mentioned Linn Energy.) This volume of insider buying may be indicating management expects positive developments going forward.

Concluding Thoughts

Natural gas' dented inventories and improving demand, both near term and further down the road, are likely to mean increased production volumes on BSM's acreage. The ensuing operational cash flow should give management the ability to return the distribution to its high water mark, with potential for more upgrades. Accommodating energy policy is likely to attract more investor interest in the natural gas complex, enhancing the potential for BSM unit price to appreciate. In sum, Black Stone Minerals' sizeable distribution offers income investors a way to be 'paid' while natural gas' power generating role evolves. And, if that unfolding is less than what's predicted, management has proven its resiliency for decades.

data.title: Amkor Technology: Near-Term Outlook Outweighs Positive Upside From AI-Related Demand
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for Amkor Technology (NASDAQ:AMKR) is a hold rating. I think the near-term outlook is very uncertain, especially with competitive pressures from ASE Technology and TSMC, margin headwinds from underutilization, and geopolitical risks. In addition, management’s weak 1Q25 guidance suggests further deterioration before any meaningful rebound. As such, I don’t see a strong reason to turn bullish today.

Business Overview

AMKR is an outsourced semiconductor assembly and test [OSAT] provider. It has exposure to multiple key end markets such as smartphones, high-performance computing [HPC], automotive, industrial, and consumer electronics [CE]. All these end markets are classified into 4 segments: Communications (48% of FY24 revenue); automotive, industrial and others [AIO] (18.5%); Computing (18.4%); and Consumer (15.2%).

4Q24 results update

AMKR 4Q24 results were very weak. Revenue fell to $1.63 billion, 7% y/y and 12% sequentially. Breaking down the segment performance: Communications revenue fell 26% sequentially (-27% y/y); AIO revenue declined 7% sequentially (-17% y/y), marking the seventh consecutive quarter of y/y declines; Computing revenue was a bright spot, up 15% sequentially and 50% y/y, driven by AI GPU and ARM-based PC demand; and lastly, Consumer revenue increased 10% y/y but was down 4% sequentially. Although gross margin did well, staying at 15.1% (highest among the four quarters in FY24), adj EBITDA was down 7% y/y to $302 million.

The real issue was guidance. Management’s 1Q25 revenue guidance calls for $1.275 billion (-7% y/y, -22% sequentially), which implies the downturn is still ongoing and, in fact, will get worse. Gross margin is also expected to contract to 11.5%, down 360 bps sequentially, reaching the lowest level in over a decade.

Challenges Holding Back Near-Term Growth

My biggest concern is the competitive pressures that AMKR is facing in OSAT and advanced packaging. In the OSAT industry, it is increasingly competitive, with ASE Technology (ASX) aggressively expanding its advanced packaging footprint, and management has guided for an aggressive growth outlook for 2025, showing that they are very serious about winning demand. In the advanced packaging space, the leader is clear—TSMC, which dominates CoWoS packaging for AI chips. The fact that TSMC is expanding capacity makes me wonder how much AMKR is able to capture demand from the ongoing AI investment cycle. A simple question is, why use AMKR when TSMC is already the one making the chip?

This competitive pressure has a direct impact on gross margins. It is not difficult to figure out why. For AMKR, utilization matters a lot because it reduces fixed costs per unit (per annual production), and this means high operating leverage as utilization goes up. Given that underlying demand is already poor, the add-on pressure from increased competition will further depress utilization. This also comes at a time when AMKR is ramping up its Vietnam facility (note: this ramp-up contributed to an 80 bps gross margin hit in 4Q24 and is expected to worsen to 100 bps in 1Q25), which may see the timeline to reach ideal utilization rates extended, therefore further pressuring margins.

Lastly, the geopolitical uncertainty is a big factor that could heavily weigh on AMKR's near-term stock performance, particularly regarding trade tensions and export restrictions on AI chips between the US and China. The latter has a more direct impact that is very worrisome. The recent tightening of AI chip regulations in China has already affected semiconductor supply chains, creating uncertainty for OSAT demand.

Despite the weakness I mentioned, there is a case for AMKR to see a rebound in demand. The upcoming launches of AI-enabled smartphones (like the Apple iPhone), improvement in the automotive industry (which is still in a downturn), and continued demand momentum (as seen in the Computing segment) for AI-driven packaging solutions for GPUs and HPC could drive up growth. For the latter point, as much as I am worried about alternative packaging methods, given that latent demand for AI chip packaging solutions (a rising tide lifts all boats), AMKR should still capture a portion of this AI-driven demand.

I also highlight that AMKR revenue has been 45% weighted toward 1H and 55% toward 2H. Management now expects 60% of 2025 revenue to come in 2H, which implies a 50% sequential growth rate in 2H25. If this happens as expected, the stock sentiment will turn incredibly bullish as it sets up a strong baseline for FY26 growth.

Valuation

Given the weaknesses I have stated, I believe AMKR's valuation multiple (~15x forward PE) is at a level where it is as good as it can get. Over the next twelve months, as per consensus estimates, revenue y/y growth is effectively muted, which contrasts with the ASX growth outlook or low-teens percentage. Despite this major delta, AMKR trades at similar levels to ASX (just a 1x multiple difference), and AMKR trades at its historical average multiple. As such, I think it is hard to justify AMKR trading higher than where it is today. If anything, this multiple suggests that there is room for multiples to de-rate lower, to potentially 6x forward PE (-1 standard deviation of AMKR trading range).

Conclusion

My view for AMKR remains neutral. The weak 1Q25 guidance and growing competition in OSAT and advanced packaging make me concerned about the near-term outlook (both revenue growth and margin). The current valuation is also at a level where I don’t think it will rerate higher. Until the stock gets cheaper, I would remain on the sidelines.

data.title: CGMS: An Attractive Opportunity In The Fixed Income Space
data.description:
Thesis

With risk-free rates high, fixed income has started to be an exciting area again, especially for non-leveraged exchange-traded funds. The active bond space has seen an explosion of choices in the past two years, with virtually all large asset managers launching active ETFs. We have covered the Capital Group U.S. Multi-Sector Income ETF (NYSEARCA:CGMS) before here, shortly after the fund was launched. In our initial article, we outlined the fund structure and holdings, and articulated our macro view on the name. We are going to revisit the ETF after more than a year of performance since our original article. Furthermore, we are going to look at it in light of the other choices in the space, and look at risk/reward analytics for the cohort.

Intermediate duration is appealing

While the jury is still out in respect to the long end of the curve, intermediate duration looks appealing at current rate levels. Kindly keep in mind that the Fed controls the front end of the curve via Fed Funds, but the market controls the intermediate and long end portions. The long end of the curve has been hit hard by deficit fears and the potential resurgence of inflation. From a duration standpoint, intermediate bonds have lower drawdowns even if risk-free rates move much higher. We are saying that intermediate bonds are appealing in today’s macrocycle since all-in yields are rewarding when providing for 5% to 6% all-in coupons with low duration and credit risk.

Holdings

The fund is overweight U.S. names:

From a sectoral standpoint corporate bonds account for most of the exposure in this ETF:

Corporate bonds, notes and loans represent 69% of the fund, followed by MBS bonds at 20% and ABS at 6.1%. The main risk factor here are therefore corporate spreads.

From a ratings perspective, the fund is overweight investment grade names, but has a sizable high yield composition as well:

When excluding unrated names, the below investment grade sleeve is roughly 39% of the fund holdings. To note that most of the HY names are clustered in the BB bucket, which is the least risky one for below investment grade names.

Performances and risk analytics

Let us have a look at CGMS's performance and its relative move versus a cohort of competitors:

We are comparing the fund to the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC), the PIMCO Multisector Bond Active ETF (PYLD) and the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE). CGMS is the clear outperformer from the cohort, and we will look at risk analytics to see if the name takes more risk than the others:

All the funds hold a mix of investment grade and high yield bonds. JPIE has the lowest duration and the lowest risk analytics as highlighted by standard deviation and volatility. The fact that the name has produced the lowest total return is thus not a surprise, since it takes the least amount of risk from the cohort.

CGMS and PYLD have similar durations, yet CGMS has managed a net superior total return, which speaks well to the fund managers and their market acumen. BINC has a slightly lower duration and slightly lower risk analytics. Kindly keep in mind though that these are active funds, and managers will shift around duration profiles and holdings as the market trades. JPIE is currently the most defensive choice here, and it has run a lower risk profile in the past years.

Advantages of active bond funds

We are of the opinion that today’s environment characterized by high volatility and lack of direction for rates is ideal for active bond funds. When yields are trending in one direction, passive funds can provide for a compelling opportunity, but when the market trades ranges with a lack of conviction, being in an active fund is a superior choice. We have seen significant fluctuations in rates in the past two years, with market participants undecided between inflationary pressures and strong economic data versus the easing monetary policy that was started by the Fed via their September 2024 rate cut.

An active fund can cut duration and credit exposures fast, thus taking shorter term views when compared to an index. Furthermore, they can bar-bell their approach to credit selection based on spread compression and market opportunities.

Macro view - corporate spreads

Corporate spreads are indeed tight, both for IG and HY bonds:

BBB spreads are currently trading at 101 bps, close to the lowest levels recorded in 2021 when rates were close to zero. While there is an argument that all-in yields are high, thus the net interest expense for corporations is higher, when at the bottom of the range there is only way direction spreads can move, and that is up.

Similarly, HY spreads for the BB sleeve are very low, even lower than the 2020/2021 period:

We think HY is even more stretched when compared to IG, considering that HY has true default probabilities embedded in the compensation.

CGMS takes mostly corporate spread risk, and via its duration we have an implied -4% drawdown for 100 bps parallel up shift in corporate spreads across IG and HY.

Conclusion

CGMS is a fixed income ETF. The name falls in the active bond category, and competes with top names from PIMCO and JPMorgan. The fund has outperformed its cohort by 200 bps in the past two years, with similar risk/reward analytics. While we are concerned about corporate spreads being stretched, CGMS' portfolio management team has proved its value via its trading acumen. We believe the most conservative choice to be JPIE for investors worried about spreads moving higher, while CGMS looks the best from the remaining names on a risk/reward basis. We like what the portfolio managers have done and believe this name is an attractive choice in today's macro environment.

data.title: Global Blue: Takeover Offer Comes At Only A Slight Premium (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis

Global Blue (NYSE:GB) announced that it has entered into an agreement with Shift4 (NYSE:FOUR), according to which Shift4 will acquire 100% of GB shares. The strategic fit is clear as Shift4 aims to add GB's merchant solutions to its global payments platform, while enhancing its features by adding GB's tax refund and currency conversion capabilities. The acquisition is expected to be closed in Q3 this year.

GB also posted impressive preliminary figures for Q3, which puts the company on track to deliver FY25 Adjusted EBITDA at the upper end of management's guidance. I would point out that I have remained bullish on GB stock since May last year when shares were trading below $5. I even reiterated my bullish stance following GB's Q2 earnings release. In this write-up I share why I think the acquisition is likely to go through despite the acquisition price only coming at a modest premium of 20% to yesterday's closing price. I am therefore downgrading GB stock to a Hold as shares are now trading close to the acquisition price.

Reviewing preliminary Q3 results

Global Blue announced preliminary figures for Q3 FY25 along with the announcement of the acquisition offer. Revenue was up 20% year over year, reaching €131 million, while Adjusted EBITDA was €52 million, up 31% year over year. Given that Adjusted EBITDA in H1 FY25 was €101.7 million, this puts the company on track to exceed €200 million in Adjusted EBITDA for FY25, which is closer to the upper range of management's guidance of €185 million to €200 million.

The Tax Free Shopping Business Update for January published by the company showed that sales continued to be strong, with 19% year over year Sales in Store growth in continental Europe and a corresponding 46% growth in Asia Pacific.

Analyzing the acquisition price and the implied valuation

The acquisition price of $7.5 per share reflects a 20% premium over the closing price on the 18th of February. This puts the company's market cap at $1.82 billion, based on 242 million shares outstanding, after also including the 40 million preferred shares. Given the company's net debt at the end of Q2 of $520 million, this implies a EV/Adjusted EBITDA valuation multiple of 11.2, based on €200 million expected for FY25, which translates to around $210 million, assuming a FX rate of 1€=$1.05.

In order to arrive at the expected FCF, I deduct $40 million in interest expenses, capex of $45 million and tax payments of approximately $30 million from the expected adjusted EBITDA figure. This leads to FCF of $95 million, implying a Price/FCF multiple of 19.1. If we exclude interest costs, to get the true FCF generation potential for the business, the valuation multiple on a Price/FCF basis drops to just 13.4, based on FCF of $135 million.

Even after the takeover premium, this still represents a very attractive valuation for this resilient business, which has a dominant market position in tax free shopping, and is expected to grow revenue at double digit rates, with potential margin upside.

Expectations looking ahead

Shift4 has $1.42 billion in cash on hand, while also having access to a 364-day $1.8 billion bridge loan facility that was entered in connection with the transaction. This makes it unlikely that they will have any issue with respect to funding. Furthermore, Shift4 estimates that approximately 90% of the owners of the GB's issued and outstanding common shares and preferred shares are willing to tender their shares. This includes large owners like Silverlake and Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) which have ownership stakes of 63% and 9%, respectively. Given the low public float of just 15%, I believe it is very likely that Shift4 ends up getting the minimum 90% acceptance that is required.

Conclusion

GB posted strong Q3 results with Adjusted EBITDA on track to be close to the upper range of full year guidance. Its valuation remains attractive at the takeover price of $7.5 per share and while the acquisition price only reflects a modest premium, the deal is likely to go through, with a low likelihood for a competing bid to arrive at this stage. With shares now trading at close to the acquisition price, I am downgrading the stock from Buy to a Hold.

data.title: Private Equity, Venture Capital Flows To Biotechnology Lowest Since 2019
data.description:
In 2024, global private equity and venture capital transaction value and volume in biotechnology declined to their lowest levels since 2019, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Compared to 2023, transaction value declined by 11% to $20.28 billion, and the number of deals dropped to 777 from 894.

Rising interest rates affecting investment valuations, increasing product development costs and a complex regulatory environment have contributed to the decline, Dan Shoenholz, US healthcare and life sciences PE leader at EY-Parthenon, told Market Intelligence.

However, the sector showed some positive data in 2024, with private equity-backed biotechnology transaction values increasing each quarter throughout the year.

The US and Canada had 281 deals in 2024, the highest among all regions, followed by the Asia-Pacific region with 264 deals.

The aggregate transaction value of private equity in biotech companies in the US and Canada reached $13.33 billion, followed by Europe at $4.41 billion and Asia-Pacific at $2.45 billion.

Largest biotech deals in 2024

All top 10 private equity-backed biotechnology deals in 2024 involved companies based in the US and Canada.

Obesity treatment therapies provider Kailera Therapeutics Inc.'s $400 million round of funding, led by Bain Capital Life Sciences Investors LLC, Lyra Capital LLC, Atlas Venture LP and RTW Investments LP, was the largest deal in 2024.

The second-largest deal was Arsenal Biosciences Inc.'s $325.4 million series C round of funding, which included participation from Arch Venture Partners LP, Mwg Management Ltd., Regeneron Ventures, NVentures LLC, Luma Group Advisor LLC, funds and accounts advised by T. Rowe Price Associates Inc., and Rock Springs Capital Management LP. Existing investors Parker Institute for Cancer Immunotherapy, SoftBank Vision Fund II-2 LP, Bristol-Myers Squibb Co., Westlake Village BioPartners, Kleiner Perkins Caufield & Byers, Byers Capital Management LLC and Hitachi Ventures GmbH also participated.

Within healthcare, biotechnology was the second-most invested industry, while pharmaceuticals had the highest deal value total of $28.15 billion. However, biotechnology stood out with the highest number of deals.

Outlook

A shrinking pool of public biopharma companies is increasing demand for strong privately held biotech companies, said Peter Kolchinsky, founder and managing partner of RA Capital Management LP, a life sciences-focused private equity firm that participated in fifty private financings in the biotech industry in 2024.

"The most promising science and the best companies are still attracting funding at competitive valuations," Kolchinsky said.

Carter Neild, managing partner at OrbiMed Advisors LLC, said, "lower interest rates and a pro-innovation FDA" are expected to support investment in the sector.

Lower rates can provide a cheaper cost of capital for expensive R&D funding and tend to make riskier assets, like biotech startups, more attractive.

Artificial intelligence is another factor. AI could significantly impact the biotech sector, and companies are exploring its uses. Last month, the FDA released draft guidance on AI in drug development, informed by over 500 AI-related submissions since 2016, highlighting AI's expanding role in drug development.

Private equity investors may view AI as an opportunity to accelerate drug discovery and the lengthy approval processes, potentially leading to faster market entry and profits, said John Newton, private equity partner at Ropes & Gray LLP. However, AI's practical application is still developing, with regulators working to ensure its effective use while maintaining drug safety and efficacy, he added.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Texas Capital Bancshares: Preferred Shares Yielding 6.7%
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Capital Bancshares (NASDAQ:TCBI) is a regional bank with a major presence in the metropolitan areas of Texas. In addition to common shares, the bank offers a preferred share (NASDAQ:TCBIO) with a current dividend yield of 6.7%. I’ve been long the preferred shares since the regional banking crisis and visited the bank’s performance last fall. Today, the bank continues to perform well, and fixed income investors should be prepared to continue to reap long-term income from the bank’s preferred shares.

Texas Capital Bancshares Financial Results

The Federal Reserve’s easing of interest rates during the fourth quarter caused Texas Capital’s yields to pull back. Asset yields dropped 37 basis points from 5.96% to 5.59% in the fourth quarter. Unfortunately, the bank’s borrowing yields declined by less than the asset yields, dropping from 4.7% to 4.37% in the fourth quarter. While the net interest spread declined, and has been on a rough trajectory since 2022, the bank has managed its leverage well and as a result, its net interest margin remains above pandemic levels.

As with yields, Texas Capital Bancshares saw the same changes to interest income and expense during the fourth quarter. Interest income declined by $15 million, while interest expense dropped by only $4 million. While the $11 million decline in net interest income (interest income less interest expense) may concern investors, net interest income remains higher than during the latter part of 2023, early 2024, and the pandemic era.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

After several quarters of loan declines, lending for Texas Capital Bancshares surged in 2024 with the bank posting three strong quarters of lending growth. In the fourth quarter, loans grew by 3.3% and for the year, lending grew by more than 10%. The bank did have a strong year for deposit growth, despite losing 2.4% of deposits in the fourth quarter. For the year, deposits grew by nearly 13%, far above what the typical commercial bank saw.

With the quarterly inconsistencies in loan and deposit growth, Texas Capital Bancshares’ loan to deposit ratio has moved between 85 and 90% over the past couple of years. With lending growth outpacing deposits in the fourth quarter, the loan-to-deposit ratio rose to 88%. The loan to deposit ratio indicates how dependent the bank is on external financing to fund loan growth. In the case of Texas Capital Bancshares, while the ratio is higher than average, it is on par with what investors can expect from a regional bank. In fact, during 2024, the bank made significant progress towards reducing its external borrowing, a move that should help earnings in 2025.

Risks to Texas Capital Bancshares

Texas Capital Bancshares’ loans are mostly classified as either commercial, mortgage finance, or commercial real estate. Commercial loans are nearly half of the bank’s loan portfolio and tend to be comprised of shorter-term loans, but the other two types have longer duration commitments and are subject to performance issues should the economy deteriorate.

Texas Capital’s nonperforming loans did grow from $81 million to $111 million in 2024 and with a smaller than average allowance for credit losses, there is a risk of poor loan performance bleeding into earnings. Since it is likely that the bank will need to increase its loan allowance in 2025, I’m not taking a position in its common shares. With the preferred shares sitting senior to the common shares, preferred share investors should not notice the volatility as much as common shareholders would.

Conclusion

Despite the risks to Texas Capital Bancshares, preferred share investors should experience less volatility to any problems that arise. The bank’s preferred share dividend is also low, which allows it to trade at a discount, and makes the possibility of them being called at par highly unlikely for the foreseeable future. Income investors can earn 6.7% dividend yield off the preferred shares with little risk of impairment, making Texas Capital Bancshares an attractive preferred share option for investors.
